summary: Introduces the concept of converting Latin-rooted English words to Spanish. Teaches the -al word ending rule, Spanish vowel pronunciation, the verb 'es' (is), and negation with 'no'.

teachings.summary: English words ending in -al tend to come from Latin and can be converted to Spanish by pronouncing them with Spanish phonetics and stressing the final syllable.
teachings.rule: Words ending in -al in English are often the same in Spanish, just pronounced with Spanish vowels and stress on the final syllable.
teachings.notes: Pronounce each vowel as written: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/. Cut out English sounds that come from nowhere (like the y in 'annual').

teachings.summary: Spanish vowels always have the same sound: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/. Unlike English where vowels have many sounds, Spanish is phonetic.
teachings.rule: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/ — always consistent.
teachings.notes: Spanish is much more phonetic than English. Pronounce letters as they are written.

teachings.summary: The word 'es' means is, and also covers he is, she is, it is, and you are (formal). No subject pronoun is needed.
teachings.rule: 'Es' = is / he is / she is / it is / you (formal) are.

teachings.summary: To negate, place 'no' before the verb. 'No' means both 'no' and 'not/don't'.
teachings.rule: Place 'no' before the verb to negate: 'No es' = it is not.

teachings.summary: English has a huge amount of Latin vocabulary that can be converted to Spanish with simple rules, giving access to around 3,000 words.
teachings.notes: Spanish is practically Modern Latin — a 'street Latin' that evolved into Spanish.

vocabulary.english: is / he is / she is / it is / you (formal) are
